text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

The fact the Dutch beach volleyball player Van de Velde, a convicted sex offender, is allowed to compete is a stain on the Olympic ideal

There was a certain, sickening irony to Steven van de Velde disembarking hisEurostar trainearlier this week, with a ring of security personnel around him. The Dutch beach volleyball player and convicted sex offender is benefitting from extra protection inParis for the Olympics, after backlash over his selection by the Dutch Olympic Committee.

He should not be in Paris at all. It should go without saying, but as a convicted child rapist he should not be permitted to compete at the Olympics.

Ten years ago, a 19-year-old Van de Velde travelled to the United Kingdom to meet a British schoolgirl, 12, and raped her. He was subsequently sentenced to four years in prison in 2016 for three counts of rape, and the judge told him it put an end to all his dreams of competing at the Olympics.

But the judge was wrong, as here we are talking about him eight years later at the most high-profile sporting event in the world.

It would be naive to think the Olympics exist as a squeaky-clean event – previousdrug scandalsand political wrangling have proven that is not the case. This still feels like a new low though.

Van de Velde served just one year in prison after being allowed to return to the Netherlands and see out his sentence under the Dutch system. He called his actions the “biggest mistake of my life” but upon his release also reportedly disputed being “branded as a sex monster, as a paedophile”. At the time, the NSPCC said his “lack of remorse and self-pity is breathtaking”.

Van de Velde has been allowed to move on from his crime and be welcomed back to the top of his sport. The Dutch Olympic Committee said he followed a “specialist treatment programme” to be deemed fit to compete for the Netherlands, and that “experts have stated that there is no risk of recidivism”.

Luckily for Van de Velde, there is nothing to block him from making his Olympic debut. Not even uproar from multiple child protection charities and a petition calling for the International Olympic Committee to disqualify him gaining more than 88,000 signatures.

His redemption arc in Paris may be absolutely disgraceful, but it is a luxury afforded time and again to male athletes accused or convicted of violent crimes against women.

For example, in football players convicted of sexual assault have found work even after landing convictions. Take Brazil starRobinho, whose part in a gang rapedid not stop three top division sides going on to sign him.

Last November a BBC investigation found that Premier League clubs had continued to play two footballers, and kept a boss in post, despite the knowledge that they were under police investigation for sexual or domestic violence.

In tennis, top star Alexander Zverev spent the past season competing at the biggest events in the world whileawaiting court proceedings for domestic violence allegations.

He denied all the charges and the case was settled out of court in June, but he was still permitted to continue playing throughout — and even reached a Grand Slam semi-final during the legal saga — because the ATP is yet to produce a domestic violence policy (which it says it has been working on for more than three years).

These cases are all separate from Van de Velde’s. But there is a common thread that runs through these stories: men in sport accused or even convicted of acting violently against women remain in prominent, public-facing positions and are even lauded for their sporting achievements. They are given the benefit of the doubt or even — in Van de Velde’s case — allowed to join an exclusive club and become an Olympian.

There may be a 50-50 split of male and female athletes at the Olympics for the first time, but a child rapist’s presence in Paris makes a mockery of the idea that we are anywhere close to achieving gender parity.

This is a Games where female athletes who choose to wear a hijab were facing obstacles earlier this week around attending the opening ceremony, yet Van de Velde is being safely escorted around the French capital and shielded from media interviews.

There was universal horror around a video of Britain’s six-time Olympic medallistCharlotte Dujardin appearing to repeatedly whip a horseduring training, released earlier this week.

She immediately and rightly withdrew when news of an investigation into her actions was made public, but Van de Velde’s proven rape against a young girl does not disqualify him from taking to the court.

Sport at this level, with millions watching, is not the place for violent men to complete their rehabilitation, but allowing them to do so somehow normalises their crimes.

In comments she later apologised for, former British athlete Paula Radcliffe even wished Van de Velde luck and effectively said second chances are afforded to drug cheats, so why not a convicted child sex offender?

According to reports, Van de Velde will not be staying atthe Olympic Village. Instead, the Netherlands Olympic Committee (NOC) have taken steps to find him alternative digs – in yet another extra accommodation made to ensure he can take part in Paris comfortably.

They released a statement on Wednesday to say they were “implementing concrete measures to ensure a safe sporting environment” for all, including “at the request of Van de Velde, alternative accommodation and no media contact during his stay in Paris”. How nice for him.

The Olympic Charter supposedly sets out the practice of sport as a human right. But becoming an Olympian should be a right reserved to those who earn it. Van de Velde’s crimes go against every ideal about Olympians serving as role models and should void any physical ability he possesses to compete.

But on Sunday Van de Velde will begin his Olympic campaign at one of the Games’ most beautiful venues, on a beach volleyball court built with the Eiffel Tower as its backdrop. Such a striking setting cannot hide the shameful stain he has already imprinted on these Olympics.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Dutch rapist's Olympic presence shows violence against women is still dismissed
author: Molly McElwee
description: The fact the Dutch beach volleyball player Van de Velde, a convicted sex offender, is allowed to compete is a stain on the Olympic ideal
